journal-title-group.journal-title: International Journal of Nursing Studies Advances

title-group.article-title: Nursing workforce challenges in gulf cooperation council countries: A systematic review of factors influencing shortage and retention

contrib-group.aff: aDepartment of Pediatric Surgery, Hamad Medical Corporation, Doha, Qatar
bFaculty of Medicine, Alexandria University, Alexandria, Egypt
cFaculty of Medicine, Al-Azhar University, Assiut, Egypt
dNursing Research Department, Hamad Medical Corporation, Doha, Qatar

permissions.copyright-statement: © 2026 The Authors. Published by Elsevier Ltd.

license.license-p: This is an open access article under the CC BY-NC-ND license (http://creativecommons.org/licenses/by-nc-nd/4.0/).

sec.title: Background/Aim
sec.p: The Gulf Cooperation Council countries are experiencing a significant nursing workforce shortage, compounded by rapid population growth, a rising burden of chronic diseases, and heavy dependence on expatriate nurses. With the World Health Organization forecasting a global shortfall of 4.5 million nurses by 2030, this review aims to systematically explore the key factors contributing to nursing workforce shortages in the Gulf Cooperation Council region and offer evidence-based recommendations for improving retention.

sec.title: Method
sec.p: A systematic review was conducted in accordance with PRISMA guidelines. A comprehensive literature search was performed across PubMed, Embase, Web of Science, and Scopus databases to identify relevant primary research published in English up to January 2025. Studies focused on nursing workforce issues in Gulf Cooperation Council countries were included. A total of eight eligible studies were critically appraised using the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale and the Critical Appraisal Skills tool.

sec.title: Results
sec.p: The findings highlight a complex interplay of factors influencing nurse shortages in the Gulf Cooperation. A poor work environment, lack of interprofessional collaboration, and inadequate leadership support were strongly associated with high turnover intention. Excessive workloads, reported by 59.4% of nurses, and emotional exhaustion, affecting over 60%, were key stressors. Additional barriers included cultural challenges, limited access to professional development, and weak organizational support systems. Leadership was identified as the most influential factor negatively correlated with nurse retention.

sec.title: Conclusion
sec.p: Addressing the nursing workforce crisis in the Gulf Cooperation Council requires multifaceted interventions. Priority areas include leadership development, structured career pathways, and improved cultural integration efforts. Further longitudinal and regionally inclusive research is recommended.

list-item.p: Gulf Cooperation Council countries face persistent nursing shortages driven by rapid population growth, chronic disease burden, and heavy reliance on expatriate nurses.
Poor work environments, high workloads, and burnout are globally recognized contributors to nurse turnover and workforce instability.
Leadership and organizational support are critical determinants of nurse retention across healthcare systems.

notes.p: Alt-text: Unlabelled box dummy alt text

list-item.p: This review synthesizes evidence from Gulf Cooperation Council countries, identifying leadership quality as the most influential factor negatively associated with nurse retention.
Emotional exhaustion, excessive workload, and weak organizational support systems were consistently linked to high turnover intention among nurses in the Gulf Cooperation Council.
The findings provide region-specific, evidence–based recommendations emphasizing leadership development, structured career pathways, and culturally responsive retention strategies

notes.p: Alt-text: Unlabelled box dummy alt text

sec.title: Introduction
sec.p: The nursing profession is a cornerstone of healthcare systems worldwide, playing a critical role in delivering quality patient care and ensuring the overall efficiency of health services (Dadheech, 2022). The global healthcare system faces challenges in maintaining enough nurses. There are currently 29 million nurses worldwide. The world health organization (WHO) predicts a shortage of 4.5 million nurses by 2030 (Boniol et al., 2022). This shortage represents a critical threat to healthcare systems' ability to deliver quality patient care and achieve optimal health outcomes (Tamata and Mohammadnezhad, 2023). Within this global context, the Gulf Cooperation Council countries face unique and complex challenges in addressing their nursing workforce needs, making it essential to understand the multifaceted nature of this crisis (Sheikh et al., 2019).

The healthcare system in the Gulf Cooperation Council region is experiencing growing demand for qualified nurses due to rapid population growth, longer life expectancy, and an increase in chronic diseases. (Khoja et al., 2017). Despite extensive investments in healthcare infrastructure and educational advancements, Gulf Cooperation Council countries encounter persistent challenges in mitigating nursing shortages, predominantly attributed to their substantial reliance on expatriate nurses (Alluhidan et al., 2020; Alsadaan et al., 2021). This has led to an unstable workforce, especially with the current global competition for nurses and geopolitical events which affect staff availability and retention (Sheikh et al., 2019).

Research has demonstrated that inadequate nursing staffing levels correlate with increased patient mortality, higher rates of medical errors, and reduced quality of care (Haegdorens et al., 2019; Nantsupawat et al., 2022). Moreover, the shortage creates a cycle of increased workload for existing staff, leading to burnout, job dissatisfaction, and ultimately, higher turnover rates (Galanis et al., 2023; Kelly et al., 2021). This situation particularly affects specialized care units and rural healthcare facilities, where staffing challenges are often most acute (Tamata and Mohammadnezhad, 2023).

Multiple interconnected factors contribute to the nursing shortage crisis in Gulf Cooperation Council countries. These include organizational elements such as challenging work environments, inadequate leadership support, and high patient-to-nurse ratios (Alharbi et al., 2020). Cultural factors also play a significant role. Nursing is often perceived as a less desirable profession in many Gulf societies, particularly among nationals, due to cultural gender norms, economic opportunities in higher-status sectors, and a lack of awareness about the profession's value, with foreign workers often filling these roles (Alharbi et al., 2020; Katoue et al., 2022). Educational barriers, including limited access to high-quality nursing education programs and challenges in clinical training capacity, further compound the problem; however, recent efforts to improve nursing education and expand clinical training opportunities have begun to address these issues (Alreshidi et al., 2021).

Understanding the causes of nursing shortages in the Gulf Cooperation Council region is essential for creating effective, sustainable solutions. This review analyzes factors affecting both national and expatriate nurses, socio-cultural influences, and workforce strategies. By identifying these causes, we can inform evidence-based policies, improve workforce planning, and develop targeted retention strategies. This approach helps address the specific needs of nurses and create culturally appropriate interventions to empower the nursing profession in the Gulf region.

sec.title: Method
sec.p: This systematic review was conducted following the PRISMA (Preferred Reporting Items for Systematic Reviews and Meta-Analyses) guidelines and Cochrane Handbook (Cumpston et al., 2019; Liberati et al., 2009).

sec.title: Data sources and search strategy
sec.p: A comprehensive search of PubMed, Embase, Web of Science (WoS), and Scopus databases was performed to identify relevant studies. The search terms for this study included keywords and geographic terms to ensure comprehensive coverage of the relevant literature. The keywords used were "nurse shortage," "nursing shortage," "nurse deficit," "nursing deficit," "nursing workforce crisis," "nurse workforce crisis," "shortage of nurses," "nursing staff shortage," and "staffing shortages." To capture studies specific to the region of interest, geographic terms included "Gulf countries," "Middle East," "Arabian Gulf," "Gulf Cooperation Council," " Gulf Cooperation Council countries," "United Arab Emirates," "Saudi Arabia," "Kuwait," "Oman," "Qatar," and "Bahrain." Boolean operators and medical subject headings (MeSH) terms were used where applicable to refine the search. The search was conducted from database inception to January 2025. The full search strategies for each database are provided in Supplementary Table 1.

sec.title: Eligibility criteria
sec.p: The inclusion criteria for this study focused on research related to healthcare facilities and organizations within Gulf Cooperation Council countries, specifically Saudi Arabia, the United Arab Emirates, Kuwait, Bahrain, Qatar, and Oman, and the nursing workforce comprising both national and expatriate nurses.

The context encompassed all healthcare settings in Gulf Cooperation Council countries, covering public and private healthcare sectors and all levels of healthcare delivery, including primary, secondary, and tertiary care. Eligible study types were primary research studies utilizing qualitative, quantitative, or mixed methods, published in English from inception to January 2025.

Studies were excluded if they were not specific to Gulf Cooperation Council countries, focused on other healthcare professions, were opinion pieces or editorials without substantive data, or were published in languages other than English.

sec.title: Study selection
sec.p: All identified records were imported into EndNote X9 software, and duplicates were removed. Titles and abstracts were screened independently by two reviewers for relevance. Full texts of potentially eligible studies were retrieved and assessed for inclusion against the predefined eligibility criteria. Discrepancies during the selection process were resolved through discussion or consultation with a third reviewer. To reduce the risk of publication and language bias, we also performed manual screening of reference lists to identify any additional studies that may not have appeared in database searches.

sec.title: Data extraction
sec.p: Data were extracted from the included studies using a standardized form. Study characteristics, such as study design, country of origin, sample size, population characteristics, key findings, mediating factors, turnover intention outcomes, and study recommendations, were systematically recorded. Additionally, participant demographics, including gender distribution, age ranges, nationality, educational background, and years of experience, were also documented.

sec.title: Quality assessment
sec.p: The quality of the included cross sectional studies was evaluated independently by two reviewers using the Newcastle Ottawa Scale (NOS) for cohort and case-control studies (Wells et al., 2013). The NOS assesses study quality across three domains: Selection, Comparability, and Outcome, with a maximum score of nine stars. Studies with a score of 7 or more stars were classified as high-quality. Regarding the included qualitative studies, the Critical Appraisal Skills Programme (CASP) tool was used to assess the qualitative study's quality (CASP Checklists—Critical Appraisal Skills Programme 2026). This tool evaluates key domains such as research aims, methodology, design alignment, data collection rigor, credibility of findings, ethical considerations, and the study's overall contribution.

sec.title: Data synthesis
sec.p: A qualitative synthesis approach was employed to analyze the extracted data, which included both quantitative metrics (e.g., staffing ratios, turnover rates) and qualitative insights (e.g., interviews and survey responses), focusing on identifying and categorizing key themes related to nursing shortages in Gulf countries. These themes included work environment and leadership factors, workload and staffing issues, cultural and communication challenges, professional development and recognition, emotional and psychological burden, organizational support and resources, compensation and benefits, work-life balance and scheduling, professional autonomy and decision-making, and physical work facilities.

sec.title: Results
sec.p:
Our search identified 467 records from PubMed (n = 78), Embase (n = 115), Web of Science (n = 34), and SCOPUS (n = 240). After removing 108 duplicates, 359 records were screened by title and abstract, with 327 excluded for relevance. Thirty-two reports underwent full-text screening, leading to the exclusion of 24 studies (conference abstracts: 6; non-Gulf region studies: 11; irrelevant: 7). Eight studies met the inclusion criteria and were included in the systematic review (Al Sabei et al., 2022; Alasiry and Alkhaldi, 2024; Alharbi et al., 2020; Alreshidi et al., 2021; Al-Shomrani et al., 2024; Alsufyani et al., 2021; Batran, 2019; Wazqar, 2018). Fig. 1Fig. 1
The PRISMA flow chart for the study selection process.Fig 1 dummy alt text

sec.title: Baseline and summary of the included studies
sec.p:
Among the included studies, seven were cross-sectional in design review (Al Sabei et al., 2022; Alasiry and Alkhaldi, 2024; Alharbi et al., 2020; Alreshidi et al., 2021; Al-Shomrani et al., 2024; Alsufyani et al., 2021; Batran, 2019; Wazqar, 2018), while one study employed a qualitative approach review (Wazqar, 2018). The included studies were primarily conducted in Saudi Arabia (Alasiry and Alkhaldi, 2024; Alharbi et al., 2020; Alreshidi et al., 2021; Al-Shomrani et al., 2024; Alsufyani et al., 2021; Batran, 2019; Wazqar, 2018) and Oman (Al Sabei et al., 2022). The sample sizes range from 14 to 2113 participants. Most studies highlight a high percentage of female nurses, with gender representation ranging from 67.4 % to 97.3 % female. Educational backgrounds predominantly feature diploma (32.0 % to 56.0 %) and bachelor's degrees (41.7 % to 82.6 %). Further details are shown in Table 1 and Table 2.Table 1
Baseline characteristics of the included studies.Table 1: dummy alt text

Foster teamwork and support systems to enhance resilience.

Encourage a supportive and motivating work environment.

Implement strategies to improve job satisfaction and reduce burnout.

Review and improve wage and benefit structures.

Communication satisfaction mediates the relationship.Communication Satisfaction79 % reported intent to leave due to poor communicationFoster effective communication strategies within teams. Improve work environment conditions to enhance satisfaction.

(2020)A descriptive correlational study with cross-sectional dataSaudi Arabia497Expatriate nursesNurse participation in hospital affairs is linked to job satisfaction and intent to leave. Staffing adequacy is linked to emotional exhaustion.Emotional Exhaustion, Job Satisfaction56.1 % reported a high intent to leaveIncrease nurses’ involvement in decision-making processes. Improve staffing levels and resource availability.

(2019)Self-administrative questionnaireSaudi Arabia213Nurses working in Intensive Care UnitsWorkload, lack of resources, and dealing with death were defined as key sources of work stress.Workload, lack of resources and support, and dealing with death-Implement stress management programs. Promote work-life balance initiatives.
Wazqar et al. (2018)A qualitative studySaudi Arabia14Oncology nursesTwo categories emerged: "extent of work stress" and "work-related stressors," with subcategories including workload, staff shortages, emotional demands, lack of support, language barriers, disrespect from patients and families, and cultural differences.Work‐related stressors-Hospitals should implement stress reduction initiatives, such as workshops and social support programs, while addressing systemic issues like workload and staff shortages, to improve oncology nurses' well-being and stress management skills in Saudi Arabia.

sec.title: Quality assessment of the included studies
sec.p: The quality assessment of the included cross-sectional studies reveals a generally high standard, with 6 studies rated as "Good" based on the Newcastle-Ottawa Scale (NOS) criteria (Al Sabei et al., 2022; Alharbi et al., 2020; Alreshidi et al., 2021; Al-Shomrani et al., 2024; Alsufyani et al., 2021; Batran, 2019), and only one cross sectional study was rated as having moderate quality (Alasiry and Alkhaldi, 2024). Also, the qualitative study by Wazqar et al. (2018) received a low bias rating across all quality dimensions using CASP tool (Wazqar, 2018). Supplementary Table 2 and 3

sec.title: Institutional environment and support
sec.p: The work environment and leadership quality emerge as crucial factors contributing to nursing shortages in Gulf countries. Studies consistently show that poor work environments correlate with higher turnover intentions. Al Sabei et al. (2022) found that the quality of interprofessional teamwork had a significant impact on nurses' intention to leave, with ineffective teamwork contributing to a higher likelihood of turnover (Al Sabei et al., 2022). The study demonstrated that each additional patient in a nurse's workload was associated with a 1.7 % increase in the likelihood of leaving the organization.

Leadership style and management support also play vital roles. Alasiry et al. (2024) revealed that transformational leadership style was most effective in reducing turnover intentions, with a correlation coefficient of −0.348, compared to transactional (−0.295) and laissez-faire (−0.258) styles (Alasiry and Alkhaldi, 2024). Alharbi et al. (2020) reinforced this finding, showing that nurse participation in hospital affairs and nurse manager ability significantly impact job satisfaction and retention (Alharbi et al., 2020).

Poor management practices and lack of support from leadership create a challenging work environment. Wazqar et al. (2018) reported that nurses frequently cited lack of social support from supervisors as a major stressor, with participants noting that "the organization only cares about work and the patient census but not cares about who provides the care and no support at all (Wazqar, 2018)"

Regarding the institutional environment, Alsufyani et al. (2021) found that nurses’ perception of the work environment strongly predicted their intention to quit (b = −0.187, p < 0.05), with communication satisfaction mediating this relationship (Alsufyani et al., 2021). Al-shomrani et al. (2024) emphasized the role of organizational resilience support, identifying a negative correlation between nurses’ intention to leave and resilience (r = −0.126, p = 0.024) (Al-Shomrani et al., 2024). Staffing and resource adequacy were also critical, with Alharbi et al. (2020) reporting one of the lowest mean scores (2.85 ± 0.67) among work environment factors (Alharbi et al., 2020).

sec.title: Workload and staffing issues
sec.p: Excessive workload and inadequate staffing levels emerge as persistent challenges driving nursing shortages. Alreshidi et al. (2021) found that workload factors had a negative impact, with a high mean score (3.7 ± 0.8, on a 5-point Likert scale) of respondents agreeing that "the amount of work interferes with how well it is done (Alreshidi et al., 2021). Wazqar et al. (2018) identified workload and staff shortage as the most common work-related stressors among oncology nurses, leading to physical and mental exhaustion. One participant noted, "Because of work overload and lack of staffing, there is no enough time to spend with each patient, to meet his/her needs and to listen to their problems (Wazqar, 2018). Similarly, Batran et al. (2019) quantified this issue, revealing that workload stress had the highest average scores among sources of work stress (59.4 ± 14.67), followed by lack of support (55.3 ± 19.13) (Batran, 2019). The study also found significant correlations between job stress and mental health problems (r = 0.448, p = 0.001).

sec.title: Cultural and communication challenges
sec.p: Wazqar et al. (2018) identified language barriers as a significant work-related stressor, with nurses reporting challenges communicating with cancer patients in certain situations (Wazqar, 2018). Cultural differences further complicated patient-nurse relationships, as family members' active involvement in patient care often interfered with information disclosure and treatment decisions (Wazqar, 2018). One participant highlighted the ethical dilemmas and added stress caused by family requests to withhold information, stating, "Physicians are not told the truth about the patient's condition and cancer treatment due to family requests, leaving me caught between the physician and my patient" (Wazqar, 2018).

sec.title: Career growth and empowerment
sec.p: Alreshidi et al. (2021) reported that professional growth, development, and recognition were highly valued by nurses, with "active staff development or continuing education" receiving the highest mean score (4.1 ± 0.7) (Alreshidi et al., 2021). However, opportunities for career advancement were often limited, and professional recognition remained a concern. Wazqar et al. (2018) highlighted that nurses often felt undervalued, with one participant stating, "Cancer patient and family in Saudi Arabia not believe in nursing and treat nurses as unprofessional people" (Wazqar, 2018). Financial recognition also appeared inadequate; Alreshidi et al. (2021) found that many respondents disagreed with the statement "extra work is acknowledged with monetary incentive" (3.1 ± 1.2), indicating insufficient compensation for additional responsibilities (Alreshidi et al., 2021).

Alharbi et al. (2020) found that nurse participation in hospital affairs received one of the lowest mean scores (2.83±0.63), indicating limited involvement in organizational decision-making (Alharbi et al., 2020). The impact of limited autonomy is particularly evident in clinical settings.

sec.title: Emotional and psychological burden
sec.p: The emotional and psychological demands of nursing work contribute to turnover intentions. Al Sabei et al. (2022) reported that >60 % of nurses experienced high levels of emotional exhaustion, with an overall average score of 30.64±.97 (Al Sabei et al., 2022).

Wazqar et al. (2018) highlighted the emotional demands in oncology nursing, where nurses reported strong emotional attachments to patients and difficulty coping with patient deaths (Wazqar, 2018). This emotional burden, combined with limited psychological support, creates a challenging work environment.

Similarly, Batran et al. (2019) identified nervousness (32.4 %) and exhaustion (30.0 %) as the most common mental health issues among nurses (Batran, 2019). A moderate correlation between mental health problems and job stress (r = 0.448, p = 0.001) highlights the significant impact of psychological burden on nurse retention.

sec.title: Compensation and benefits
sec.p: Al-shomrani et al. (2024) found that nurses earning >10,000 SAR (2666 USD) reported higher turnover intentions, suggesting that salary alone may not be sufficient for retention without other supporting factors (Al-Shomrani et al., 2024). The study revealed notable associations between turnover intention and monthly allowances, highlighting the intricate link between compensation and job satisfaction. Moreover, Alreshidi et al. (2021) reported that wages and benefits played a crucial role in staff turnover, as many respondents disagreed with the statement that "extra work is acknowledged with a monetary incentive" (3.1 ± 1.2) (Alreshidi et al., 2021). Additionally, nurses with higher education levels and greater experience expressed higher intentions to leave.

The relationship between compensation and nationality also emerges as significant. Alharbi et al. (2020) noted that nurses educated in India were much less likely to intend to leave compared to nurses educated in other countries (e.g., Saudi Arabia or Philippines) (OR = 0.42, p < 0.01), potentially indicating different expectations and perceptions of compensation adequacy across nationalities (Alharbi et al., 2020).

sec.title: Work-Life balance and scheduling
sec.p: Alreshidi et al. (2021) identified interpersonal relationships and leisure time as key factors influencing nurses' satisfaction, with participants emphasizing the importance of "maintaining interpersonal relationships, and having a proper distribution of working hours and days off" (3.6 ± 0.9) (Alreshidi et al., 2021). Further, Al-shomrani et al. (2024) indicated that 45.6 % of participants worked 12-hour shifts, and 58.5 % of those on rotating day, evening, and night shifts experienced heightened stress (Al-Shomrani et al., 2024).

The impact of long working hours and irregular schedules extends beyond the workplace. Batran et al. (2019) found that physical health problems such as back pain (71.4 %) and headache (69.5 %) were common among nurses, potentially exacerbated by demanding schedules and insufficient rest periods (Batran, 2019).

sec.title: Physical work environment and facilities
sec.p: Alreshidi et al. (2021) stated that hospital facilities were generally considered inadequate, with basic systems available like "contacting on-call staff and physicians" receiving modest ratings (3.3 ± 1.1) (Alreshidi et al., 2021). Housing facilities and services for nurses were also recognized as notable concern. Alreshidi et al. (2021) reported disappointing scores for housing facilities, with issues ranging from recreational opportunities to basic amenities like food service. The lowest score (2.8 ± 1.3) was for "variety and balance diet food items provided," indicating substandard living conditions for nursing staff (Alreshidi et al., 2021). Further, Batran et al. (2019) emphasized the prevalence of physical health problems among nurses, suggesting that insufficient facilities and equipment could contribute to occupational health issues and drive higher turnover intentions (Batran, 2019). All the aforementioned factors are concisely summarized in Table 2.

sec.title: Economic, policy, and migration influences on the nursing workforce
sec.p: In addition to organizational and cultural contributors, our review highlights key systemic and economic factors influencing nursing shortages in the Gulf Cooperation Council. Several studies highlight the heavy reliance on expatriate nurses in Saudi Arabia (Alharbi et al., 2020; Alreshidi et al., 2021), which affects workforce stability due to turnover intentions influenced by cultural adjustment, work environment, and remuneration. Nationalization policies aim to increase the proportion of Saudi nurses, but substantial changes will take years. Wage and benefits are critical drivers of retention, with dissatisfaction linked to turnover intentions alongside workload and management factors (14). Moreover, global competition for skilled nurses from countries such as India and the Philippines shapes workforce composition and retention, emphasizing the need for policies that address both national and international labor dynamics (Alharbi et al., 2020; Alreshidi et al., 2021).

sec.title: Discussion
sec.p: Our systematic review revealed several interconnected factors contributing to nursing shortages in Gulf Cooperation Council countries, which can be arranged according to their significance within the context of Gulf Cooperation Council culture. Specifically, during the COVID-19 pandemic in Saudi Arabia, frontline nurses experienced high levels of burnout and emotional exhaustion, with increased working hours and fear of infecting family contributing significantly to stress and workforce challenges (Alsulimani et al., 2021; Mary Pappiya et al., 2023).

First, the work environment emerged as a crucial determinant of nursing shortages. Poor interprofessional teamwork and inadequate leadership support significantly increased turnover intentions among nurses. This finding aligns with global studies, which emphasize the importance of a supportive work environment in retaining nursing staff (Tamata and Mohammadnezhad, 2023). However, the cultural dynamics in the Gulf Cooperation Council, where hierarchical structures often influence communication and teamwork, may exacerbate these issues (O’Daniel and Rosenstein, 2008). Leadership, has shown a strong negative correlation with turnover intentions in our review, is particularly vital in the Gulf Cooperation Council context, as it fosters a supportive environment that can mitigate the challenges posed by traditional structures (Gan and Voon, 2021).

Second, workload and staffing issues emerged as persistent challenges. Studies reported high mean scores for work interference, with significant correlations between job stress and mental health problems. This is consistent with findings from Yu et al. (2019), which highlight the negative impact of job demands on nurse resilience globally (Yu et al., 2019). However, the rapid population growth and increasing prevalence of chronic diseases in the Gulf Cooperation Council create a unique context where the demand for nursing services is particularly acute, leading to higher stress levels. (30).

Third, the emotional and psychological burden faced by nurses was particularly evident, over 60 % experiencing high levels of emotional exhaustion. This finding aligns with global literature, such as Jun et al. (2021), which links nurse burnout to decreased patient safety and quality of care (Jun et al., 2021). However, the cultural context in the Gulf Cooperation Council, where familial ties and community expectations may strength emotional demands on nurses, adds a layer of complexity not typically seen in Western healthcare settings (Sheikh et al., 2019). The emotional attachment to patients highlights the need for tailored psychological support systems that consider these cultural nuances (Molina-Mula and Gallo-Estrada, 2020).

Fourth, Cultural and communication challenges, including language barriers and family involvement in patient care, introduce unique regional complexities. Wazqar et al. (2018) highlight how these factors create ethical dilemmas for nurses (Wazqar, 2018). In Gulf Cooperation Council countries, traditional gender norms and societal attitudes shape the nursing workforce, limiting career progression and deterring nationals, particularly women, from entering or remaining in the profession (Elmorshedy et al., 2020; Ennis and Walton-Roberts, 2018). The need for comprehensive cultural competency training and language support services is critical in the Gulf Cooperation Council, where a significant portion of the nursing workforce comprises expatriates from diverse backgrounds (Sheikh et al., 2019).

Fifth, the paradox of limited professional development opportunities. While global studies, such as those by Şenol Çelik et al. (2024), emphasize the importance of career advancement in nurse retention, the Gulf Cooperation Council context reveals a gap between the perceived value of professional growth and the actual opportunities available (Şenol Çelik et al., 2024). This discrepancy may arise from the evolving status of the nursing profession in the region, where recognition and advancement pathways are still being established.

Our review highlights key challenges faced by nurses in the Gulf Cooperation Council, which can be interpreted through conceptual frameworks. For example, limited professional growth, inadequate recognition, and poor organizational support relate to Herzberg’s Motivation Theory (Herzberg, 1966), affecting job satisfaction and turnover intentions. Systemic and institutional challenges align with the WHO Workforce Retention Framework, illustrating how leadership, staffing policies, and resource allocation influence retention (Figueiredo et al., 2025).

Studies highlight key approaches to addressing nursing staff shortages, including retention enhancement, recruitment diversification, and international staffing (Cameron et al., 2004; Zulfiqar et al., 2023). Retention strategies encompass empowering nurses in decision-making processes (Laschinger and Finegan, 2005), promoting work-life balance through flexible scheduling (Fukuzaki et al., 2021), and fostering positive organizational culture (Kiptulon et al., 2024). Research emphasizes the importance of fair compensation (Mamikhani et al., 2013) and structured career progression through preceptorship programs (Powers et al., 2019). Recruitment initiatives focus on diversifying the workforce demographic (Phillips and Malone, 2014), while international recruitment requires careful ethical consideration regarding source country impacts (Stokes and Iskander, 2021).

sec.title: Strengths and limitations
sec.p: This systematic review demonstrates several notable strengths. To our knowledge, it is the first systematic review conducted in the Gulf Cooperation Council region to address nursing shortages. Our comprehensive search strategy encompassed multiple databases, ensuring a thorough examination of available evidence. Additionally, the quality assessment of included studies using standardized tools (NOS and CASP) enhances confidence in the reliability of our findings.

However, some limitations warrant consideration. First, the predominance of studies from Saudi Arabia may limit the generalizability of findings to other Gulf Cooperation Council countries. Second, the reliance on cross-sectional studies prevents the establishment of definitive causal relationships between identified factors and nursing shortages. Additionally, while some Gulf Cooperation Council countries, are seen as having good healthcare systems, the lack of comprehensive data on nursing conditions limits our understanding of overall trends and potential disparities in shortages across the region. Moreover, our review did not cover all nursing units, which raises concerns about differences in shortages across different specialties, or sittings such as inpatient and outpatient. Furthermore, publication bias and the restriction to English-language studies may have excluded relevant Arabic literature, potentially limiting the comprehensiveness of our review. Finally, the limited number of included studies and heterogeneity in designs, outcomes, and assessment tools precluded quantitative synthesis, restricting the ability to estimate the magnitude of influence for different factors.

sec.title: Conclusion and future recommendations
sec.p: This systematic review provides evidence on the complex nature of nursing shortages in Gulf Cooperation Council countries, highlighting the interplay of organizational, cultural, and professional factors. Based on our findings, we recommend implementing comprehensive retention strategies as part of a multifaceted approach to addressing nursing staff shortages, which includes retention enhancement, recruitment diversification, and international staffing. These strategies are primarily targeted at policymakers for policy formulation, hospital administrators for workforce planning and management, and nursing educators for professional development initiatives. Further, developing leadership programs, establishing structured career advancement pathways, and enhancing cultural integration support systems should be considered. Given that most available studies originated from Saudi Arabia, the findings should be interpreted with caution when applied to other Gulf Cooperation Council countries with limited published evidence. Future research must focus on longitudinal studies examining the long-term effectiveness of retention interventions that require further investigation, particularly in underrepresented Gulf Cooperation Council countries, and across a broader spectrum of hospital sectors.

sec.title: Funding
sec.p: The publication of this article will be funded by the Qatar National Library, Qatar.

sec.title: Data availability statement
sec.p: No data has been generated in this study.

sec.title: CRediT authorship contribution statement
sec.p: Amani N.Al. Ansari: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology, Investigation, Conceptualization. Alaa Ahmed Elshanbary: Writing – original draft, Project administration. Mohamed Sayed Zaazouee: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft, Methodology. Kalpana Singh: Writing – review & editing, Writing – original draft.

sec.title: Declaration of competing interest
sec.p: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.
